attributes.stem: A 75-year-old woman visits her general practitioner two months after undergoing a total hip replacement surgery secondary to a traumatic fall. She does not report any pain and has been following up consistently with the prescribed physiotherapy routine and rehabilitation. However, she complains of difficulty walking since the surgery, where she notices that she she needs to shift her weight from side to side, particularly on the right side. On physical examination, her gait shows sagging of the left pelvis when her right leg is weight-bearing, and there is pelvic drop on the left side when she is asked to stand on her right leg.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which nerve supplies the affected muscle group?
attributes.explanation:
It is most likely that the right superior gluteal nerve has been injured. This nerve innervates the right gluteus minimus and medius, which are responsible for hip abduction. This can be demonstrated as a positive Trendelenburg sign, where the pelvis drops on the left side when the patient stands on the right leg, due to inability to maintain a level hip. Furthermore, injury to this nerve would result in a Trendelenburg gait, where patients will shift their weight to the affected side in order to bring the contralateral leg forward.

The left femoral nerve innervates the hip flexor and knee extensor muscle groups. With regards to this nerve, the muscle responsible for hip flexion is the iliopsoas, and the muscles responsible for knee extension are the quadriceps. Injury to the femoral nerve would cause difficulty lifting the thigh when climbing steps or walking from iliopsoas weakness, and knee buckling from quadriceps weakness. Furthermore, it would not cause a positive Trendelenburg sign, and sensory loss would be present over the anterior and medial thigh, which is not seen in this patient.

The left sciatic nerve innervates the posterior compartment of the thigh which are responsible for hip extension, namely biceps femoris, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, and the ischial portion of the adductor magnus. Although this nerve is at risk of injury from a hip replacement surgery utilising the posterior approach, it would result in significant impairment in hip extension, and would not present with a positive Trendelenburg sign.

The right obturator nerve is responsible for hip external rotation and hip adduction, namely the obturator externus, gracilis, pectineus, adductor magnus, adductor longus, and adductor brevis. Injury to this nerve would cause sensory loss at the medial thigh, as well as difficulty performing side-to-side movements. Trendelenburg sign would also not be positive in this condition, and it does not correlate to the patient's symptoms above.

The left inferior gluteal nerve innervates the left gluteus maximus muscle and is responsible for hip extension movements. Injury to this nerve would cause significant impairment in gait, particularly a lurching gait, as hip extension is crucial in the stance phase of the gait cycle. However, injury to this nerve would not cause a positive Trendelenburg sign and the patient's symptoms are not consistent with this nerve injury.
learningPoints: Positive Trendelenburg sign - Pelvis drops on the contralateral side when the patient stands on a single leg on the affected side.
Trendelenburg sign is used to demonstrate hip abductor weakness (gluteus medius and minimus), which can be a complication of hip replacement surgery.
references: Hardcastle P, Nade S. The significance of the Trendelenburg test. J Bone Joint Surg Br. 1985 Nov;67(5):741-6. doi: 10.1302/0301-620X.67B5.4055873. PMID: 4055873. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/4055873/

Gogu S, Gandbhir VN. Trendelenburg Sign. 2022 Nov 14. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan–. PMID: 32310447. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32310447/

Pai VS. Significance of the Trendelenburg test in total hip arthroplasty. Influence of lateral approaches. J Arthroplasty. 1996 Feb;11(2):174-9. doi: 10.1016/s0883-5403(05)80013-0. PMID: 8648312. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8648312/
